This is internally inconsistent — a lower Cramér–von Mises statistic indicates closer proximity to uniformity, so the baseline's W² is the favorable one. The causal inference connecting PIT shape to the calibration claim is therefore broken. Additionally, the '≈30% improvement' figure is an unsupported aggregation: σ_NMAD yields ~22% relative gain ((0.027−0.021)/0.027) while η_0.15 yields ~34%; neither alone nor together self-evidently rounds to 30%.",

rationale.R1.2: Verifying evidence-claim alignment inherently requires tracing the logical chain from reported statistics to stated conclusions. A critical logical failure exists here: the artifact reports W²=0.084 for FlowPhot vs. W²=0.031 for the baseline, then asserts the 'favorable W² value for FlowPhot' confirms better calibration. This is internally inconsistent — a lower Cramér–von Mises statistic indicates closer proximity to uniformity, so the baseline's W² is the favorable one. The causal inference connecting PIT shape to the calibration claim is therefore broken. Additionally, the '≈30% improvement' figure is an unsupported aggregation: σ_NMAD yields ~22% relative gain ((0.027−0.021)/0.027) while η_0.15 yields ~34%; neither alone nor together self-evidently rounds to 30%.
rationale.R1.3: This is the explicit and sole load-bearing task of the delegation. Every evidentiary element must be checked: (a) Table 2 scalar comparisons (η_0.15: 3.1% vs. 4.7%; σ_NMAD: 0.021 vs. 0.027) against the headline calibration claim; (b) the PIT / Figure 4 analysis — the W² values directly contradict the stated interpretation (FlowPhot W²=0.084 > baseline W²=0.031, yet the artifact claims FlowPhot's value is 'favorable' and supports the calibration claim; this is a direct evidence-claim misalignment); (c) Table 3 faint-regime figures (σ_NMAD 0.029 vs. 0.034) against the claim of retained improvement; (d) the '~30% calibration improvement' summary against the individual statistics it purports to synthesize.
